content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 2 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TH_002_page_001.jpg, 1TH_002_page_002.jpg

**2:1** For you yourselves know, brothers, that our coming to you was not in vain,

**2:2** but having previously suffered and been shamefully treated in Philippi, as you know, we were bold in our God to speak to you the good news of God amid much opposition.

**2:3** For our exhortation does not spring from error, nor from impurity, nor from deceit,

**2:4** but just as we have been approved by God to be entrusted with the good news, so we speak — not to please men, but God, who examines our hearts.

**2:5** For we never at any time came with a word of flattery, as you know, nor with a pretext for greed — God is witness —

**2:6** nor seeking glory from men, neither from you nor from others, though we could have claimed authority as apostles of Christ.

**2:7** But we were gentle in your midst, like a nursing mother tenderly caring for her own children.

**2:8** So deeply did we care for you that we were delighted to share with you not only the good news of God but our very selves, because you had become beloved to us.

**2:9** For you remember, brothers, our labor and toil: working night and day so as not to burden any of you, we proclaimed to you the good news of God.

**2:10** You are witnesses, and God also, how devoutly and justly and blamelessly we conducted ourselves toward you who believe,

**2:11** just as you know how, like a father with his own children, we were exhorting each one of you and encouraging and charging you,

**2:12** that you should walk worthily of God, who calls you into his own kingdom and glory.

**2:13** And for this reason we also give thanks to God unceasingly, that when you received the word of God that you heard from us, you accepted it not as the word of men but as what it truly is — the word of God, which is also at work in you who believe.

**2:14** For you, brothers, became imitators of the assemblies of God that are in Judea in Christ Jesus, because you also suffered the same things from your own countrymen, just as they did from the Jews,

**2:15** who killed both the Lord Jesus and the prophets, and drove us out, and do not please God, and are contrary to all men,

**2:16** hindering us from speaking to the nations so that they might be saved, so as always to fill up the measure of their sins. But the wrath has come upon them at last.

**2:17** But we, brothers, having been bereaved of you for the time of an hour — in person, not in heart — were all the more eager with great longing to see your face.

**2:18** For we wished to come to you — indeed I, Paul, again and again — but Satan hindered us.

**2:19** For what is our hope, or joy, or crown of boasting? Is it not you yourselves, before our Lord Jesus at his coming?

**2:20** For you are our glory and our joy.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

This chapter presented the council with Paul's most personally revealing rhetoric in 1 Thessalonians — dense with familial metaphor (nursing mother, father, orphan), self-defense of apostolic conduct, and a theologically charged passage on divine wrath. The three translators produced broadly harmonious renderings, reflecting the relative clarity of Sinaiticus for this chapter, but diverged meaningfully in register, word choice, and interpretive framing across nearly every verse.

**On register and audience.** PNEUMA consistently modernized the language ("pointless," "threw our weight around," "blocked our path," "torn away," "desperate"), producing a vivid, contemporary English text. EMET preserved Greek syntax almost intact, yielding translation-English that reads as a parsing exercise ("the entrance of us, the [one] toward you"). LOGOS occupied the formal middle ground. SOPHIA's consensus draws most heavily from LOGOS for overall sentence structure, while selectively adopting PNEUMA's clarity where EMET and LOGOS produced unnecessarily stiff English, and honoring EMET's precision where the Greek carries theological weight.

**On the textual variant in verse 7.** All three translators rendered "gentle" (HPIOI) in their final text, though PNEUMA's notes incorrectly state that Sinaiticus reads NHPIOI ("infants") — in fact, the manuscript clearly reads HPIOI without the initial nu. EMET and LOGOS correctly identified the Sinaiticus reading. The consensus follows the manuscript: "gentle."

**On "assemblies" for ekklesia.** All three translators independently chose "assemblies" over "churches," maintaining consistency with the project's translation philosophy. SOPHIA affirms this rendering.

**On verse 16 (eis telos).** EMET rendered "unto the end," LOGOS "to the uttermost," and PNEUMA "finally caught up with them." SOPHIA adopts "at last," which captures the aorist force of ephthasen (the wrath has already arrived) and the temporal/completive sense of eis telos without overcommitting to a single theological interpretation.

**On the familial metaphor arc.** LOGOS rightly noted Paul's sustained familial imagery: nursing mother (v.7), father (v.11), orphaned (v.17). SOPHIA preserves this arc by using "bereaved" in v.17 (following LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "torn away," retaining the familial resonance of aporphanisthentes.

**Credit.** LOGOS provided the structural backbone for most verses. PNEUMA's dynamic phrasing improved verses 7-8 and 16. EMET's literalism served as the indispensable check against over-interpretation, particularly in verses 13, 15, and 19.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
